title: The complete AI Video Maker guide
description: Models, aspect ratios, durations, seeds, start and end frames — every control in the AI Video composer, with the prompts and settings that actually work.
body:
The AI Video Maker takes a single sentence and gives you back a finished shot. This guide walks through every control in the composer — the ones you should usually touch, and the ones to leave alone until you know what you want.

The model picker

Lumen ships with five video models, each with a personality:

  • Veo 3.1 Lite — the cheap, fast default. Great for drafting until your prompt feels right.
  • Veo 3.1 Pro — cinematic 1080p with native synced audio. Pick this for hero shots.
  • Sora 2 — the strongest physical-world simulation. Pick this when objects need to behave correctly.
  • Runway Gen-4 — best when you want granular motion control and editor-style workflows.
  • Kling 3 — the best price-per-second for long takes.

If you don't know which to pick, draft on Veo 3.1 Lite, then re-render the keepers on Veo 3.1 Pro or Sora 2 once your prompt is right. Drafts are roughly 5× cheaper.

Writing prompts that land

A useful AI video prompt has three parts:

  1. Subject — what is in the frame. Be specific. Not “a cat,” but “a tabby kitten with one folded ear.”
  2. Action — what they're doing. “Yawning slowly” reads completely differently from “stretching its back.”
  3. Cinematography — the look. Lens (“35mm”), light (“golden hour, soft rim light”), motion (“slow dolly in”), and style (“painterly, soft Ghibli watercolour”).

An example prompt

A tabby kitten with one folded ear, yawning slowly on a windowsill at golden hour, soft rim light, dust motes drifting, 35mm shallow depth of field, painterly Ghibli watercolour style.

Aspect, duration, resolution

  • 9:16 for TikTok, Reels, Shorts.
  • 16:9 for YouTube and landscape ads.
  • 1:1 for feed posts.
  • 21:9 for cinematic intros (Veo 3.1 Pro only).

Duration affects coherence: 4-second clips are nearly always coherent. 8 seconds becomes risky on prompts with many moving parts. 12 seconds is where you need to lean on Kling or Sora 2.

Resolution maps cleanly to credits: 720p costs roughly half what 1080p does. Draft in 720p, finalise in 1080p.

Start and end frames

Tap the Start frame slot to pin the first frame of your video to an image from your camera roll. This is the single best trick in Lumen — uploading a still image essentially gives you image-to-video, which is far more controllable than text alone.

End frame is rarer but powerful: combine start and end frames to specify exactly where the camera needs to begin and end.

Audio mode

Veo 3.1 ships with native audio — ambience, music, or even dialogue if your prompt suggests it. The audio toggle in the composer turns this on or off:

  • Off — silent video, mix sound later (recommended for ads).
  • Ambient — natural soundscape (wind, room tone).
  • Music — generates a fitting score in the model's voice.
  • Dialog — synthesises speech for any quoted text in your prompt.

Seed and variations

If you find a generation you like and want close variations, tap the dice to lock the seed and bump variations to ×2 or ×4. Same prompt, same seed, slight differences — like negatives in film photography.

A 60-second workflow

  1. Open Image & Video.
  2. Tap the green Video tab.
  3. Type a 1-sentence subject with one cinematography note.
  4. Pick 9:16, 4 seconds, 720p, Audio off.
  5. Tap the up-arrow.
  6. Iterate the prompt 2-3 times until you like it.
  7. Lock the seed, bump to ×4, re-render on Veo 3.1 Pro at 1080p.

That's it. Five minutes from idea to shareable.

title: Ship your first faceless video in 5 minutes
description: A full walkthrough from blank screen to a 9:16 short, fully narrated, captioned and scored — the whole faceless YouTube and TikTok pipeline in one tap.
body:
Faceless videos are a quiet superpower. No camera, no makeup, no edit suite — and yet the most viewed short-form videos of the last year are almost all faceless. Lumen's Faceless Video flow collapses the whole pipeline into one screen.

The shape of a faceless video

Every great faceless short follows the same skeleton:

  • Hook — first 2 seconds, makes you not scroll.
  • Promise — what you'll learn or feel.
  • Payoff — the actual content, 3-5 beats.
  • Loop or CTA — last second sets up the rewatch or the follow.

Step 1 — Idea

Open the Faceless flow and type a single sentence describing your topic. Lumen will turn it into a full narrated script. Two examples that consistently produce good scripts:

3 surprising habits of focused engineers.
What if Tokyo had no neon — three calmer Asian capitals to fall in love with.

Tip: the more concrete your sentence, the more grounded the script. “Productivity tips” is too broad; “Productivity tips for shift-workers who sleep odd hours” is gold.

Step 2 — Pick your voice

Tap the Voice row. The picker filters by accent, gender and vibe. For faceless videos that need to feel personal, pick a voice with the warm or casual tag. For documentary-style, the cinematic or documentary tags.

Step 3 — Pick a B-roll style

This is the look:

  • Cinematic — real-looking footage, shallow depth of field. Default.
  • Animated — motion graphics. Great for tech.
  • Documentary — handheld, natural light.
  • Ghibli — soft painterly watercolour. Pick this when the topic is emotional or whimsical.

Step 4 — Captions

The default Kinetic caption style boosts watch-time by 20-30% on platforms with sound-off feeds (Reels, TikTok). For YouTube Shorts, Bold performs nearly as well and is less distracting.

Step 5 — Aspect & music

9:16 for Reels/TikTok/Shorts. Leave Auto background music on — Lumen's score sits underneath the narration without ducking it badly.

Generate

Tap Generate. Stages flow past — “polishing script” → “casting voice” → “selecting B-roll” → “burning captions” → “stitching scene.” A 40-second video typically lands in 60-90 seconds.

Post and iterate

Open the result, hit Share, and post directly. Then in the Projects tab, tap the saved project and duplicate it — same script, different B-roll style, different voice. You'll learn which combinations land for your audience faster than any course.

title: Create your AI avatar from a single photo
description: Turn one clear photo (or 15-second clip) into a studio-grade talking-head video that speaks any language with natural gestures and lip-sync.
body:
AI Avatars used to take half a day of studio recording. With Lumen's Avatar V model (the same family as HeyGen's flagship), you need one clear photo and a script. That's it.

Pick a reference

Two options work best:

  • A single high-quality photo — well-lit, facing the camera, eyes visible. Avoid sunglasses, hats that shade the eyes, or strong side light. Phone selfies near a window are perfect.
  • A 15-second clip of you talking — better for long-form, because Lumen captures your real gestures and energy as the foundation.

Write a script that sounds like you

Read it out loud first. If you wouldn't actually say it, the avatar will sound stilted. Use contractions, short sentences, and occasional CAPS to emphasise words — Avatar V is audio-driven, so emphasis in your typed script becomes emphasis on the avatar's face.

Voice + language

Pick a voice that matches the energy. For 175+ language coverage, Lumen automatically cross-renders the voice in the language you select — so a friendly American voice can deliver the same script in Japanese with the same warmth.

Background and motion

  • Studio — soft cream, neutral.
  • Office — for B2B content.
  • Cafe / Garden — warmer settings, great for personal content.
  • Transparent — alpha-channel export, drop into any video editor.

Motion options are Still, Subtle, or Expressive. Subtle is the right default for almost everything. Use Expressive only for high-energy content like ads or sales pitches.

Generate and review

A 30-second avatar renders in roughly 90 seconds. Your first generation is for review — pay attention to: hand gestures (do they distract?), eye contact (does the avatar look at the right spot?), and mouth shapes (do the lips genuinely match the audio?).

Tip — drive realism with audio

The single biggest lever for avatar quality is the audio you feed it. A monotone audio = a flat avatar. A warm, expressive read = a warm, expressive avatar. If your generations feel lifeless, re-record the voiceover in Lumen with the same voice but more energy in the read.

title: Storyboard a multi-shot scene with consistent characters
description: Plan a sequence of shots in advance, attach a character reference, and let Lumen generate every cut with a coherent look — the way real shoots are planned.
body:
Single-shot AI videos are magical, but real stories need cuts. The Storyboard tool lets you plan 3-12 shots in sequence and generate them with a coherent look and consistent characters.

When to reach for Storyboard

Use Storyboard whenever your idea has a beginning, middle and end — even a tiny one. A 12-second ad with three cuts. A 30-second “day in the life.” A music-video chorus. Anywhere a single shot would feel like a fragment.

The reference image is the secret

Upload one image to the Character / style reference slot at the top. This single image teaches Lumen what your protagonist looks like, what the lighting looks like, what the colour palette is. Every shot below will pull from it.

Best references are clean, well-lit, single-subject images. A portrait, a hero product shot, or a colour-graded establishing frame.

Writing the shot list

For each shot, write a short prompt — one or two sentences. Lean on the reference for who and what, lean on your prompt for the action and camera. Example:

Shot 1 (4s): The protagonist walks into a sun-bathed bakery, soft tracking shot from behind.
Shot 2 (3s): Close-up on her hands picking up a croissant, warm light.
Shot 3 (4s): Wide reverse, she takes a bite and smiles, golden hour through the window.

Pace your shot lengths

Short shots feel energetic. Long shots feel calm. A useful rule: at least one shot in your sequence should be twice the length of the shortest. This creates rhythm.

Aspect consistency

Pick one aspect ratio for the whole storyboard. Mixing aspects mid-sequence almost never reads well.

Generate

Generation runs sequentially — Lumen finishes shot 1 before starting shot 2, so you can cancel partway if a shot isn't landing and re-write it.

After generation

Lumen sequences the shots automatically with subtle 4-frame dissolves. If you want a hard cut instead, export the individual shots from the project detail screen and assemble in your editor of choice.

title: Auto captions that actually look good
description: Style, position, highlight colour, font size, emoji suggestions — the small details that make the difference between captions that work and captions that fight your video.
body:
Eighty-five percent of social video is watched with the sound off. Your captions are your video. Here's how to get them right.

Pick the right style

  • Minimal — small white text, no fill. Pairs with cinematic, polished content. Doesn't fight the footage.
  • Bold — chunky text with a soft drop-shadow. The safe default for talking heads.
  • Kinetic — words pop in one at a time with subtle bounces. Highest retention on TikTok and Reels.
  • Karaoke — full sentence with a moving highlight on the current word. Best for music videos and slower content.
  • Subtle — soft cream text on a soft cream background. Beautiful for moody, atmospheric work.

Highlight colour

The highlight colour is the small block behind your current word. Use it to match your brand or your content's mood:

  • Peach / amber — warm, indie, friendly.
  • Mint / meadow — wellness, lifestyle, calm.
  • Lavender — beauty, fashion, dreamy.
  • Rose — feminine, romance.

Position

Default Bottom works for most content. Switch to Middle for hero shots where you want the caption to feel like a graphic, not a subtitle. Top only works on vertical platforms where your subject is in the lower two-thirds.

Size

On vertical phone video, default to M. Use L only when the caption is the focal point (single-word reveals). S is for landscape and YouTube long-form.

Emoji suggestions

Lumen can sprinkle subtle emojis per phrase, matched to the meaning. Turn this on for casual, high-energy content and off for documentary or business.

Languages

Lumen captions in 30+ languages. If your audio is in English but you want Spanish captions, run AI Dub first to produce Spanish audio, then generate captions on the dubbed video.

title: Dub a video into 12 languages without losing your voice
description: Translate any video while preserving the original speaker's voice and emotion, with optional lip sync. The complete dub workflow, with the settings that matter.
body:
Dubbing used to cost more than the original production. Today, with the right model and the right settings, you can translate a 5-minute video into 30+ languages in an afternoon — keeping your voice across all of them.

Upload the source

Quality of dub follows quality of source. The cleaner your audio, the better Lumen can transcribe, translate and re-synthesise. If your source has heavy background music, run Voice Isolator on it first to extract just the dialogue, then re-mix the music underneath the dub.

Source & target language

Pick what's spoken in the video and what you want it translated to. Lumen auto-detects but trusting the picker is faster.

Voice cloning

Leave Voice cloning on. This is what keeps the dub feeling like you. Lumen samples your original voice and re-renders the translated audio in the same timbre, pacing and emotion.

If your video has multiple speakers, bump Speakers up — Lumen will diarise (separate them), clone each one, and keep each one's voice consistent in the target language.

Preserve background

This separates your dialogue from your music and ambience, dubs only the dialogue, then re-layers everything underneath. Leave it on. Without it, the dub flattens your soundscape.

Lip sync

The most expensive setting and the most impressive. Lip sync re-renders the mouth shapes of every face in the video so they match the new language perfectly. Use it for hero shots, B2B videos, and anything where viewers will be watching faces. Skip it for fast-cut social shorts where it's overkill.

What to expect

A 1-minute dub with cloning + preserve background + lip sync takes roughly 90 seconds and costs ~18 credits. Without lip sync, costs drop to ~10 credits and runtime to ~50 seconds.

Iterate by language

If the first dub feels stiff, the issue is almost always the source script being translated too literally. Lumen translates idioms culturally where it can, but rare phrases or product names can throw it. Try generating with a slightly cleaned-up version of your transcript as the source.

title: Add a precision voiceover to any video
description: Multi-segment narration with start and end times, a different voice per segment, and music ducking that doesn't fight the read.
body:
Voiceover differs from Faceless Video in one important way: you bring your own footage. Lumen lays narration over it, perfectly timed, with the music tucked underneath.

Upload footage

Any aspect ratio works. The narrative track is created independently and re-merged at the end.

Plan your segments

A segment is one continuous narration. Most short videos have 2-5 segments, separated by visual beats. Sketch them out:

  • Segment 1 (0:00 - 0:05): hook.
  • Segment 2 (0:08 - 0:15): promise.
  • Segment 3 (0:20 - 0:32): payoff.

Leave gaps between segments. Silence is part of the narration.

Voice per segment

This is the secret weapon. Pick one warm voice for the hook and a different, more authoritative voice for the payoff — your video will feel like a conversation between two friends. Or pick a calm voice for explanation and an energetic voice for the call to action.

Duck background music

Leave this on. Lumen drops the music by 9dB whenever a segment plays, then brings it back up. Sounds professional, takes zero effort.

Remove original audio

Use this when your footage has unwanted sound — dialogue you're replacing, wind noise, distracting background. The original sound is removed and only your narration + music remain.

title: Score your video automatically
description: Genre, mood, intensity, instrumentation — pick a vibe and Lumen writes a melody that breathes with your cuts.
body:
Music is half a video. If your footage feels flat, the answer is almost always sound, not picture. The Video to Music tool generates an original score that follows the rhythm of your edits.

Genre & mood

Pick a genre that matches the world of your video and a mood that matches the feeling. The combinations matter more than you'd think — “Cinematic / Melancholic” gives you something totally different from “Cinematic / Triumphant.”

Intensity

Lower intensity gives more space; higher gives more movement. For talking-head and vlog content, choose Low or Medium. For ads and transformation reveals, High.

Instrumentation

Pick 2-4 instruments. Fewer instruments mean more space — which usually means better. Piano + Strings is a forever-winner for emotional content. Synth Pad + Drums is great for tech and lifestyle.

How it reads your cuts

Lumen analyses your video for cut points (when scenes change) and aligns musical hits to them — so reveals land on beats and dissolves don't fight the rhythm.

title: Pick the right voice and dial it in
description: Stability, similarity, style — the three sliders in Lumen's text-to-speech that change everything. With examples of what each one does.
body:
The voices in Lumen are the same studio-grade voices you'd pay for in the biggest TTS platforms. The three sliders below them are what most people miss.

Stability

Stability controls how consistent the voice is across the read. Lower stability means more emotional range — pauses, sighs, energetic accents. Higher stability means a steadier, more predictable read.

  • 0.3 — expressive, theatrical. Audiobooks, dialogue, storytelling.
  • 0.5 — balanced. The default for most uses.
  • 0.8 — flat, consistent. Tutorials, news, voiceover.

Similarity

Similarity controls how closely the output matches the original voice sample. Higher similarity preserves more of the original voice's quirks — accent thickness, breath patterns, micro-pauses.

For most purposes, leave this at 0.75. Bump it to 0.9 if you're cloning your own voice and want maximum resemblance.

Style exaggeration

This one is subtle. At 0 it neutralises the voice's personality. At 1 it cranks the voice's defining traits to 11. For most voices, leaving this at 0.1-0.2 produces the most natural read. Push it to 0.5+ only for characters that should feel exaggerated.

Speaker boost

A small CPU bump that improves resemblance to the original. Worth turning on for hero content.

Writing for TTS

Use ellipses for thinking pauses. Use em-dashes for sharp breaks. Use commas generously. Avoid abbreviations — write “Doctor” instead of “Dr.” because TTS reads punctuation literally.

title: Write a full song from a single prompt
description: Eleven Music, Suno v5.5, Udio v1.5 — which model to pick, when, and how to write a prompt that gives you a real song instead of a loop.
body:
Three models live inside Lumen's AI Music tool, each with a personality:

  • Eleven Music v1 — the highest quality, trained on licensed data. Pick this for commercial work. Strongest vocals.
  • Suno v5.5 — the fastest. Catchy pop, rock and hip-hop structures. Cheap drafts.
  • Udio v1.5 — the cleanest instrumental separation. Best for jazz, electronic, ambient, classical. Longest extensions.

The prompt

Describe the song in three layers: genre, mood, and references. Example:

A dreamy lo-fi track with soft female vocals about late-night trains and quiet cities, gentle jazz piano underneath, in the vein of early 2000s indie.

Style tags

Tags are the model's grammar. Pick 4-8 from the suggested list. Genre tag + 1 mood tag + 1 vocal tag + 1 instrument tag is a strong default. Avoid stacking too many — over-tagging dilutes the model's focus.

Instrumental or vocals

If you need underscore for video, generate instrumental — vocals fight narration. If the song is the product, generate with vocals.

Custom lyrics

When you write your own lyrics, use the structure tags Lumen ships with:

[Verse]
Whispered lanterns drift along the wire
Nobody waves but I am known

[Chorus]
Hold the light, hold the light
Long enough to find your way home

Duration

30-60 seconds for ads and shorts. 90-180 seconds for songs you'll release. The model needs at least 90 seconds to deliver a proper verse-chorus-verse structure.

title: Design any sound from text
description: Cinematic SFX from a description — prompts, duration, looping, and the small craft of writing for the sound model.
body:
Lumen's SFX generator writes new sound from a description — perfect for hard-to-find foley, custom ambience, or designed transitions.

How to prompt

Be sensory and specific. Rain gives you generic rain. Heavy rain on a tin roof at night, with soft distant thunder, kept indoor gives you something cinematic.

Duration

2-4 seconds for hits and transitions. 8-12 seconds for atmospheric beds. The model's output gets thinner past 15 seconds — for long ambience, generate 8-second loops and stitch them.

Loop mode

Turn this on for ambience you'll use as a bed. The model adjusts the head and tail so they cross-fade cleanly.

Prompt influence

Higher values stick closer to your words, lower values give the model creative license. Start at 0.5; if you're getting drift, push to 0.7.

title: Make a multi-voice podcast without recording a take
description: Speaker turns, voice casting per character, pacing — the full long-form audio workflow, with the prompts and structure that make Lumen sound like a real conversation.
body:
Long-form Audio is built for podcasts, audiobooks, dialogue and bedtime stories. Multiple speakers, each with their own voice, taking turns.

Title and structure

Give your episode a clear title — the title shapes how Lumen paces transitions. Two-speaker conversational structure is the default; add a third speaker for round-tables or three-narrator audiobooks.

Cast your voices

Tap each speaker to assign a voice. Pick voices that contrast — different accent, different timbre, different gender if appropriate. Contrast is what makes a conversation feel alive.

Write turns, not paragraphs

Short turns sound natural. Long, monologue-style turns sound like reading. Aim for 1-3 sentence turns most of the time, with the occasional longer paragraph for substance.

Use natural conversation moves

Real conversations have:

  • Interruptions — “Wait, hold on—”
  • Agreement noises — “Yeah, exactly.”
  • Self-corrections — “Well actually, I think—”
  • Tangents — “Speaking of which, I read…”

Sprinkle these in. Lumen's model handles them naturally.

Generate and review

A 10-minute podcast renders in about 90 seconds. If a turn sounds wrong, swap that speaker's voice or rewrite the turn — don't re-render the whole episode.

title: Swap one voice for another while keeping the performance
description: Speech-to-speech voice replacement that preserves rhythm, emotion and timing. The exact settings to dial in for clean results.
body:
Voice Changer takes any audio of someone speaking and re-records it in a different voice — keeping the original pauses, emotion, pitch contour and emphasis.

When to use it

  • You recorded a great take but want a different voice.
  • You want to anonymise a real interview.
  • You're prototyping a character and want to test multiple voices on the same performance.

Source quality matters

The model preserves performance. If the source is mumbly, the output is mumbly. If the source is well-recorded with clear diction, the output sings.

Preserve original style

At 0, you get a clean delivery in the target voice. At 1, you keep heavy inflection from the source. Default 0.6 is the sweet spot — character without colour-bleed.

Remove background noise

Always on, unless your source is already studio-clean.

title: Clean noisy audio in seconds
description: Three modes — keep vocals, keep instrumental, or full cleanup — and the one slider that decides quality vs character.
body:
Voice Isolator pulls vocals from background noise — or background from vocals — with surgical precision. The model is the same one ElevenLabs uses internally for dubbing prep.

Three modes

  • Keep vocals — strips everything else. Use for podcast prep, dubbing source, transcription.
  • Keep instrumental — strips the vocals out of a music track. Use for karaoke, remixes, scoring.
  • Clean speech — removes background noise without separating stems. Use on phone recordings, field interviews, voicenotes.

Aggressiveness

Higher values remove more — but can colour the result, especially on speech with heavy reverb. Start at 0.6. If voices sound thin, drop to 0.4.

title: Transcribe accurately in 90+ languages
description: Scribe v2 with word-level timestamps, speaker diarisation, and four output formats. The complete transcription workflow.
body:
Lumen's transcription is powered by Scribe v2 — the same engine ElevenLabs uses for real-time captioning. It handles 90+ languages with state-of-the-art accuracy.

Pick the right format

  • Plain text — for notes, summaries, sharing.
  • SubRip (.srt) — the universal subtitle format. Use for video editing.
  • WebVTT (.vtt) — for HTML5 video players.
  • JSON — for programmatic processing.

Timestamps

Sentence-level is enough for editing and summarising. Word-level is what you need for karaoke captions, music videos, or alignment work.

Speaker diarisation

Turn this on for interviews, panels and any audio with multiple voices. Lumen labels each speaker (Speaker 1, Speaker 2…) — you can rename them in the project after.

Punctuation

Always on, unless you're processing transcripts further with your own NLP.

title: Translate a video with perfect lip sync
description: Re-record any video into a new language while keeping the speaker's face, voice and gestures intact. The HeyGen-class video translation workflow.
body:
This is the workflow that made HeyGen famous and is now in Lumen. Upload a video in one language, get back the same video — same person, same gestures, same voice — speaking a different language.

How it differs from AI Dub

AI Dub replaces audio. Video Translate replaces audio and mouth shapes. Use Video Translate when you'll see the face. Use AI Dub when audio is enough.

Setup

  1. Upload your source video.
  2. Pick source language (or let auto-detect).
  3. Pick target language.
  4. Leave Lip-sync, Preserve voice, and Enhance speech all on.

Speaker count

If multiple people speak, Lumen needs to know how many. Diarisation is automatic but the count helps the model split correctly. For most one-person videos, leave at 1.

Cost & runtime

Roughly 18 credits per source minute, ~60 seconds to render a 30-second clip. The lip-sync pass is the most expensive part — turn it off if you don't need it and you'll cut cost in half.

title: Lip-sync any audio to any face
description: Precision vs Speed mode — when to pick each one, plus the small audio prep that makes lip-sync 2× better.
body:
Lip Sync takes a video with a face and an unrelated audio file, and re-renders the mouth shapes so they match the new audio perfectly.

Use cases

  • You re-recorded narration and need the face to match the new audio.
  • You want a real person's face to deliver a script they didn't record.
  • You translated audio and the original mouth shapes don't match the new language.

Precision vs Speed

Precision mode uses HeyGen's avatar-grade lip-sync model. Higher quality, ~2× slower, ~2× the credits. Use for hero content.

Speed mode is for drafts and batch work. Quality is still high — for content that won't be watched on a big screen, it's often indistinguishable.

Audio prep

The cleaner the audio, the better the sync. Run Voice Isolator on your audio first if there's background noise. Aim for a tight, dry voice track.

Captions option

Lumen can burn captions from the new audio directly into the synced video. Good for accessibility and for sound-off feeds.

title: Extend any clip naturally
description: Continue the motion, loop for seamless ambience, or stretch a hero shot. The Video Extender, used right.
body:
Video Extend predicts the next seconds of any clip — like an autocomplete for motion. It's the simplest fix when a generation ends just a little too soon.

Plain continuation

Leave the prompt blank and the model continues the existing motion. Useful when a 4-second clip needs to be 6 to fit your edit.

Directed continuation

Add a prompt and the model steers the extension toward it. Example: a clip of a person walking, with the prompt “turns and looks at the camera, smiles” — Lumen will continue the walk and add the turn.

Loop mode

When loop is on, the model engineers the last frame to match the first frame of the source. The result loops seamlessly — perfect for ambient backgrounds and scroll-stoppers.

Extra seconds

2-4 seconds extend cleanly. 6+ seconds risks the model drifting. For anything longer than 4, use Storyboard instead.

title: Cut your subject from any video
description: Transparent, green screen, or AI-replaced background — and how to get a clean edge on every shot.
body:
Background Remover does frame-by-frame rotoscoping at the speed of a coffee. Three output options:

  • Transparent — exports with alpha channel. Drop into any video editor and place on a new background.
  • Green screen — exports with a clean green key. For editors that need a chroma source.
  • Replace with AI — Lumen generates a new background from a prompt and composites it under your subject in one step.

Edge feather

Higher feather = softer edge. Lower = harder cut. Default 0.4 is balanced. Push to 0.7 for shots with frizzy hair or fur. Drop to 0.2 for graphic-style sharp cuts.

What works best

Single-subject shots, clean lighting, good colour separation between subject and background. Lumen handles crowds too, but the edges get softer.

title: Remove, replace, or restyle anything in a video
description: The video inpainting brush — three modes, frame-by-frame mask refinement, and the prompts that produce believable results.
body:
Inpaint is the most powerful tool in Lumen, and the most demanding. Brush over any region in any frame and choose to remove it, replace it with something else, or restyle it.

Three modes

  • Remove object — paints the region with background. Use for power lines, distractions, errant tourists.
  • Replace object — paints the region with a new object from your prompt. Use for product swaps, sign changes, prop replacement.
  • Restyle region — keeps the object but changes its appearance. Use for colour changes, style transfer, look development.

Mask carefully

The brush opens after upload. Mask the first frame, the last frame, and 1-2 frames in between — Lumen interpolates the rest. The closer your masks track the subject, the cleaner the result.

Brush size

Use small brushes for precise objects (faces, signs). Use larger brushes for vague regions (skies, walls).

Prompts

For Replace mode: describe what should be there, not what's currently there. A vintage red bicycle leaning against the brick wall beats not a car.

title: One-tap viral effects, used well
description: Melt, crush, explode, glow — when each of Lumen's effects works, when it doesn't, and the intensity that lands.
body:
Lumen's effects are the same family Pika made famous. They look ridiculous in a great way and they're built for one-tap virality.

The catalog

  • Melt — your subject melts dramatically. Great for product reveals and reaction videos.
  • Crush — a heavy invisible weight smashes the subject flat. Pair with comedic timing.
  • Explode — controlled detonation. Use on objects you want to destroy “for fun.”
  • Inflate — subject puffs up like a balloon.
  • Levitate — gentle floating. The most cinematic of the bunch.
  • Paint — brushstrokes wash over the subject.
  • Cake-ify — slices through to reveal cake inside (yes, really).
  • Crystallize — turns to crystal in place.
  • Glow — radiates light. Subtle, hero-shot quality.

Intensity

0.6 is the safe default. Below 0.3 the effect is barely visible. Above 0.85 it starts breaking the source. Stay in the middle for finished work; push extremes only for jokes.

title: Pick the right image model
description: Flux, Imagen 3, Stable Diffusion 3.5, Midjourney v7 — when to reach for each, and how to combine them with style and character references.
body:
Four models live in Lumen's Image Generator. Pick by what you're making:

  • Flux 1.1 Pro — photorealism, sharp typography, perfect text rendering. The default workhorse.
  • Imagen 3 — best faces, best text. Pick for portraits and posters.
  • Stable Diffusion 3.5 — open and flexible, the cheapest. Pick for high-volume drafting.
  • Midjourney v7 — art direction, painterly look. Pick for editorial, cover art, anything where the look matters more than the realism.

Style references

Upload a single image that captures the visual world you want — colour palette, lighting, materials. Lumen will pull from it without copying it.

Character references

Upload a single image of a face or character. Lumen keeps that character consistent across every variation.

Aspects, count, seed

Default count of 4 gives you good variety. Lock the seed once you find a generation you like and re-render variations — same seed, slightly different prompts.

title: Invent a new voice from a description
description: Describe a voice that doesn't exist yet. Lumen designs it, you save it, and it's available across every audio tool.
body:
Voice Design lets you author a voice that doesn't exist yet. Describe what you want — Lumen synthesises a voice from scratch, plays you a preview line, and saves it to your library if you like it.

How to describe a voice

Three layers: person, style, texture. Examples:

A warm middle-aged American narrator with a touch of gravel, speaks at a measured pace with quiet authority.
A young French woman with bright energy, speaks quickly with light laughter underneath.

Gender, age, accent

These are seeds for the model. Pick the closest match — your prompt fine-tunes from there.

Pace & pitch

Default 0.5 is neutral. Drop pace for solemn, raise for energetic. Drop pitch for authority, raise for warmth (within reason).

Preview line

Always test with the actual content you'll use the voice for. A voice that sings audiobook narration may not work for ads. Change the preview line to something representative.

title: Find the perfect community voice
description: 1,000+ voices, filtered by source, gender, and tag. The fastest way to discover a voice you'll actually keep.
body:
The Voice Library is the discovery layer for voices. Search, filter, preview, and add any voice to your projects.

Filter strategically

  • Trending sorts by recent use — these are voices that creators are picking right now.
  • Community shows voices shared by other creators — wider stylistic range than the designed catalogue.
  • Designed shows the curated Lumen catalogue — quality-vetted, every voice tested across languages.

Tag chips

The tag row below the filters cross-cuts: narration, cinematic, casual, storybook, documentary, energetic, soft, deep, warm, anime. Combine a category filter with a tag for surgical discovery.

Preview before you commit

Tap the play button. Spend 30 seconds previewing 5 voices before you choose. The right voice changes the whole feeling of your content.

title: Build a voice agent in an evening
description: System prompt, voice, knowledge base, temperature — the four levers that turn a chatbot into a believable, useful, human-feeling conversational agent.
body:
Voice Agents are conversational AIs that can be called or embedded. Lumen wraps ElevenAgents so you can build one without writing code.

The four levers

  • System prompt — defines who the agent is and what it should do. Most important.
  • Voice — defines how the agent sounds. Pick one your callers will want to listen to.
  • Knowledge base — what the agent knows. Paste in product docs, FAQs, or your bio.
  • Temperature — how creative vs focused. Lower for support agents, higher for creative companions.

Writing a system prompt

The best system prompts have four parts:

  1. Identity — who you are. (“You are a calm receptionist for an indie design studio.”)
  2. Goal — what you're trying to accomplish. (“Help callers schedule consultations.”)
  3. Rules — what you must and must not do. (“Always offer to email a confirmation. Never quote prices.”)
  4. Style — how you talk. (“Use short, warm sentences. Never use jargon.”)

Greeting

The greeting is the first line the agent says when a conversation starts. Make it short, warm, and end with a clear question that prompts the user to speak. Bad: “Hello, I am the AI assistant. How may I help you today?” Good: “Hi, thanks for calling. What can I help you with?”

Knowledge base

Paste documents directly. The agent will retrieve from them when relevant. Don't dump everything — quality beats quantity. A focused 5-page brief outperforms a 200-page dump.

Temperature

0.2-0.4 for support and FAQ agents (stay on-script).
0.5-0.7 for sales and onboarding (be flexible but reliable).
0.8-1.0 for creative companions and storytellers (be inventive).

After you train

Lumen gives you three deployment options: call (phone number), embed (web widget), or API (your own app). Start with embed — it's the fastest to test and the cheapest to run.

title: Add a beautiful audio player to any article
description: Paste a URL, Lumen produces an embeddable player that narrates the article in any voice. The five-minute path to a 10× longer time-on-page.
body:
Audio Native turns any article into a narrated audio version with a tiny, brand-matched player you embed at the top of the page. Readers who don't have time to read can press play and walk away.

Setup

  1. Paste the article's full URL.
  2. Pick a narrator voice — warm, conversational voices outperform formal ones for blog content.
  3. Pick the player's accent colour — match your site's primary.
  4. Decide on auto-play. Most browsers block it without user gesture, so leave it off and let readers choose.

Generate

Lumen reads the article, narrates it, masters the audio, and gives you an embed snippet. Drop the snippet at the top of your post.

What changes

Across 300+ blogs using audio narration, average time-on-page goes up 30-60% on articles with a player, and bounce rate drops. Readers who would have skimmed start listening.

Costs

Roughly 1 credit per 1,000 characters of article. A typical 1,200-word post costs ~7 credits.

title: Keep characters consistent across shots
description: The two reference slots — style and character — and the small craft of using them together to maintain identity across an entire video sequence.
body:
The single hardest problem in AI image and video is consistency — keeping the same character across shot after shot. Lumen exposes two reference slots that, used together, solve most of it.

Style reference

An image that captures the visual world — colour, lighting, materials, mood. Lumen pulls the look without copying the content. Best style references are clean, high-contrast, and consistent in palette.

Character reference

An image that captures a face or a creature. Lumen preserves identity — facial features, body shape, distinctive marks — while letting the prompt control pose, clothing, environment.

Using both together

Pair a soft watercolour style reference with a portrait character reference, and Lumen will give you a Ghibli-style version of your character — same person, painted style. This is the cheat code for consistent storyboards.

Tips

  • Front-facing portraits as character references almost always outperform side profiles.
  • Style references work better when they're a single mood, not a collage.
  • If your character drifts across shots, the character reference is usually the fix — not the prompt.
